content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Above is the high-level architecture of my platform.
In my previous two articles, I shared how this project evolved and why I decided to build it. In this article, I’d like to talk about the design decisions behind the platform and some of the lessons I learned along the way.
Each color in the diagram represents a different architectural layer.

• Apps layer contains components that directly use the platform’s capabilities. These are expected to change frequently as requirements evolve. At the moment, this layer includes the React UI, data ingestion functions, and response handling. In the future, it will also include different AI agents.

• Services layer contains reusable capabilities that shouldn’t belong to any single application. Currently it includes the Rust gateway and embedding service, while components such as routers and memory systems will likely be added later.

• Deploy layer consists of infrastructure components rather than business logic.

• Outside of these layers are global configuration files such as docker-compose.yml, base_config, and .env.
My goal is to keep the platform itself straightforward and stable, while allowing users to freely plug in, replace, or remove applications depending on their own needs.

How the layers emerged
This layered architecture didn’t exist from the beginning.
Initially, I organized the project based on the order in which I built it. For example, the Rust gateway and Qdrant lived in the same layer simply because they were developed around the same time. As the project grew, two problems became obvious.
The first was configuration management. Parameters were scattered throughout the project, making troubleshooting unnecessarily difficult. Different components occasionally required different dependency versions, and finding compatible combinations often meant changing configuration values in multiple places. It also became increasingly difficult to extend the platform — for example, supporting multiple model providers or ingesting additional data sources such as websites, datasets, or personal notes.
The second, and more important problem, was coupling. Different components became tightly connected, making it difficult to replace or remove individual pieces. If I wanted to replace my UI or completely remove the existing ingestion pipeline, I couldn’t do it cleanly.
These issues eventually led me to perform two major refactorings. I reorganized the project according to responsibilities and expected maintenance frequency, centralized environment configuration, and intentionally left extension points for future capabilities.

Why not use an existing framework?
While restructuring the project, I repeatedly asked myself another question:
Why not simply build the platform on top of frameworks such as LangChain or LangGraph, or use visual workflow platforms like Dify?
My reason for building the platform myself wasn’t because those tools are lacking. They are powerful and can provide impressive functionality, solve many engineering problems, and save an enormous amount of development time.
But I still wanted to understand what the architecture should be before deciding which abstractions should come from a framework.
We’re currently in a period where the foundations of AI infrastructure are still rapidly evolving. Today’s frameworks represent excellent engineering work, but I don’t believe we’ve reached the final form of how AI systems should be built.
I don’t want to hand over the architecture of what may eventually become my own AI assistant entirely to a framework before I understand where my own system boundaries should be.
I’d rather first understand how the pieces fit together, where responsibilities belong, and how a platform should be designed to better protect both individuals and enterprises. Once those principles are clear, choosing frameworks becomes an engineering decision rather than an architectural one.
Looking back, I also noticed how my own thinking evolved throughout the project.
At the beginning, I was simply trying to get everything connected.
During debugging sessions, I started thinking about maintainability and extensibility.
Eventually, I realized that the most important question wasn’t how to connect components, but rather what each component should be responsible for — and equally important, what it should not be responsible for.

Why organize the project based on responsibilities and change frequency instead of technology?
A good example is SQLite and Qdrant.
At first, I treated them both as databases.
Later I realized they play completely different roles.
SQLite stores the source of truth — facts that should persist for a long time.
Qdrant stores vector representations of those facts, optimized for retrieval, reranking, and model inference. Its contents are disposable because they can always be regenerated from the original data.
As long as the source data remains in SQLite, the vector database can always be rebuilt. This is important because changing embedding models often requires regenerating all vector embeddings. Different embedding models may produce vectors with different dimensions, and even when the dimensions match, they usually represent different embedding spaces.
I’ve even started thinking that as the platform grows, perhaps Qdrant should only cache the most frequently accessed information, while less frequently used data could be embedded on demand. That would avoid storing duplicate representations unnecessarily.
Similarly, placing the React UI and SQLite in the same layer has nothing to do with them using similar technologies. Instead, they’re grouped together because they’re both application-specific components that can easily be replaced.
Someone could completely ignore my React UI and ingestion pipeline, connect their own frontend to the Rust gateway, populate Qdrant with their own embeddings, and still reuse the rest of the platform.

Why a gateway?
I also spent a long time deciding whether the platform even needed a gateway.
When a system only has one entry point and one model provider, a gateway can easily feel like over-engineering.
But once a second provider, a second agent, or a second application appears, the Rust gateway starts becoming much more than an API proxy — it becomes the boundary of the system.
In the Challenges & Learnings section of my GitHub README, I mention “service boundary design and refactoring for maintainability”. This will become increasingly important as the platform evolves.
For example, if I ask the current system, “Which model are you using?”, it may try to retrieve the answer from the indexed documents instead of answering based on its own runtime state.
A gateway can provide the right boundary for distinguishing platform knowledge from retrieved knowledge. It gives each component a clearer responsibility and allows the system to reason within the correct context instead of searching everywhere indiscriminately.

One design decision took much longer than I expected: logging.
It turned out that building the features was easier than making the system observable.
I’ll share those lessons in my next article.

If this project or the ideas behind it resonate with you, feel free to explore the code on GitHub, open an issue, or simply share your thoughts. I’d love to learn from different perspectives as the platform continues to evolve.
